entries.meaning: i.e., "at will" or "at one's pleasure". This phrase, and its Italian (beneplacito) and Spanish (beneplácito) derivatives, are synonymous with the more common ad libitum (at pleasure).

znacenje.hr: Latinska fraza koja označava da se nešto čini prema nečijoj slobodnoj odluci ili diskreciji, bez stroge obveze ili unaprijed zadanog pravila; često se koristi u pravnom i administrativnom jeziku.

entries.author: test

entries.meaning: i.e., "from top to bottom", "all the way through", or "from head to toe". See also a pedibus usque ad caput.

znacenje.hr: Označava cjelovitost, potpunost ili iscrpno razmatranje nečega, od početka do kraja.

entries.meaning: i.e., "on the contrary" or "au contraire". Thus, an argumentum a contrario ("argument from the contrary") is an argument or proof by contrast or direct opposite.

znacenje.hr: Metoda zaključivanja u pravu i logici: ako pravilo vrijedi za jedan slučaj, zaključuje se da za suprotan slučaj ne vrijedi.

entries.meaning: A long time ago; from Gaius Lucilius, Satires VI, 284

znacenje.hr: Aluzija na mitološki potop; koristi se za označavanje nečega što potječe iz vrlo davne prošlosti.

common_translation.en: to set forth from false principles

entries.meaning: Legal phrase. From Cicero, De Finibus IV.53.

znacenje.hr: Označava pogrešan pristup rasuđivanju u kojem su početne pretpostavke netočne, pa su i zaključci nužno pogrešni.

entries.meaning: i.e., "even more so" or "with even stronger reason". Often used to lead from a less certain proposition to a more evident corollary.

znacenje.hr: Argumentacijski izraz kojim se naglašava da, ako nešto vrijedi u slabijem slučaju, još više vrijedi u jačem.

common_translation.en: from the greater to the smaller

entries.meaning: From general to particular; "What holds for all X also holds for one particular X." – argument a fortiori

znacenje.hr: Logički zaključak: ako vrijedi za nešto veće ili teže, vrijedi i za manje ili lakše.

common_translation.en: from the smaller to the greater

entries.meaning: An inference from smaller to bigger; what is forbidden at least is forbidden at more ("If riding a bicycle with two on it is forbidden, riding it with three on it is at least similarly punished.")

znacenje.hr: Obrnuto od prethodnog; ako nešto ne vrijedi u manjem slučaju, onda sigurno ne vrijedi u većem.

entries.meaning: i.e., "completely", "from tip to toe", "from head to toe". Equally a capite ad calcem. See also ab ovo usque ad mala.

znacenje.hr: Slikovit izraz za potpunu obuhvatnost ili cjelovitost.

entries.meaning: "From possibility to actuality" or "from being possible to being actual".

znacenje.hr: Filozofski izraz koji označava prijelaz od potencijalnog prema stvarnom.

entries.meaning: Based on observation, i. e., empirical evidence. Opposite of a priori. Used in mathematics and logic to denote something that is known after a proof has been carried out. In philosophy, used to denote something known from experience.

znacenje.hr: Zaključivanje koje se temelji na opažanju, iskustvu ili posljedicama.

entries.meaning: Presupposed independent of experience; the reverse of a posteriori. Used in mathematics and logic to denote something that is known or postulated before a proof has been carried out. In philosophy, used to denote something is supposed without empirical evidence. In everyday speech, it denotes something occurring or being known before the event.

znacenje.hr: Zaključivanje koje polazi od općih načela ili pretpostavki, prije iskustva.

entries.phrase: a solis ortu usque ad occasum
entries.normalized: a solis ortu usque ad occasum
lemma: a solis ortu usque ad occasum

znacenje.hr: Označava cijeli dan ili potpun vremenski raspon.

entries.meaning: Said of an argument either for a conclusion that rests on the alleged absurdity of an opponent's argument (cf. appeal to ridicule) or that another assertion is false because it is absurd. The phrase is distinct from reductio ad absurdum, which is usually a valid logical argument.

znacenje.hr: Argumentacijska metoda kojom se pokazuje da neka tvrdnja vodi do besmislenih ili proturječnih zaključaka.

entries.phrase: ab abusu ad usum non valet consequentia
entries.normalized: ab abusu ad usum non valet consequentia
lemma: ab abusu ad usum non valet consequentia

common_translation.en: The inference of a use from its abuse is not valid
common_translation.hr: iz zlouporabe ne slijedi nevaljanost uporabe

entries.meaning: i.e., a right is still a right even if it is abused (e.g. practiced in a morally/ethically wrong way); cf. § abusus non tollit usum.

znacenje.hr: Pravna i moralna maksima: činjenica da se nešto zloupotrebljava ne znači da je sama stvar loša.

entries.meaning: Literally, "from the everlasting", "from eternity", or "from outside of time". Philosophically and theologically, it indicates something, e. g., the universe, that was created from outside of time. Sometimes used incorrectly to denote something, not from without time, but from a point within time, i.e. "from time immemorial", "since the beginning of time". or "from an infinitely remote time in the past")

znacenje.hr: Označava nešto što postoji bez početka, izvan vremena.

entries.meaning: i.e., from ancient times

znacenje.hr: Upućuje na staru tradiciju ili davno ustanovljenu praksu.

entries.meaning: Regarding or pertaining to correspondence. Ab epistulis was originally the title of the secretarial office in the Roman Empire

znacenje.hr: U Rimskom Carstvu naziv za dužnosnika koji je vodio službenu korespondenciju.

entries.meaning: Legal term denoting derivation from an external source, as opposed to a person's self or mind—the latter of which is denoted by ab intra.

znacenje.hr: Označava vanjski izvor ili utjecaj.

entries.meaning: Also sometimes written as "abhinc"

znacenje.hr: Koristi se za označavanje polazišta, mjesta ili izvora.

entries.meaning: i.e., "from the bottom of my heart", "with deepest affection", or "sincerely". Attributed to Julius Caesar.

znacenje.hr: Izraz iskrene, duboke i unutarnje osjećajnosti.

entries.meaning: Neo-Latin for "based on unsuitability", "from inconvenience", or "from hardship". An argumentum ab inconvenienti is one based on the difficulties involved in pursuing a line of reasoning, and is thus a form of appeal to consequences. The phrase refers to the legal principle that an argument from inconvenience has great weight.

znacenje.hr: Argument koji se poziva na nepraktične ili nepoželjne posljedice neke odluke.

entries.meaning: i.e., "from the beginning" or "from infancy". Incunabula is commonly used in English to refer to the earliest stage or origin of something, and especially to copies of books that predate the spread of the printing press c. AD 1500.

znacenje.hr: Označava nešto što traje od samih početaka, od djetinjstva.

entries.meaning: i.e., "from the outset", referring to an inquiry or investigation. Ab initio mundi means "from the beginning of the world". In literature, it refers to a story told from the beginning rather than in medias res ('from the middle'). In science, it refers to the first principles. In other contexts, it often refers to beginner or training courses. In law, it refers to a thing being true from its beginning or from the instant of the act, rather than from when the court declared it so. Likewise, an annulment is a judicial declaration of the invalidity or nullity of a marriage ab initio: the so-called marriage was "no thing" (Latin: nullius, from which the word "nullity" derives) and never existed, except perhaps in name only.

znacenje.hr: Često u pravnom jeziku za nešto što je nevaljano ili započeto pogrešno od samog početka.

entries.meaning: i.e., from a (dead) decedent, who died without executing a legal will; cf. ex testamento

znacenje.hr: Pravna oznaka za nasljeđivanje kada nema oporuke.

entries.meaning: i.e., from the inside, as opposed to ab extra ("from without").

znacenje.hr: Označava unutarnje razloge, motive ili procese.
Čin izvršen bez pristanka osobe.

entries.meaning: More literally, "from/by an angry man". Though the form irato is masculine, the application of the phrase is not limited to men. Rather, "person" is meant because the phrase probably elides homo ("man/person"), not vir ("man"). It is used in law to describe a decision or action that is motivated by hatred or anger instead of reason and is detrimental to those whom it affects.

znacenje.hr: Označava postupanje pod utjecajem ljutnje.

entries.meaning: i.e., from the origin, beginning, source, or commencement; or, "originally". Root of the word aboriginal.

znacenje.hr: Upućuje na izvor ili ishodište nečega.

entries.meaning: i.e., from the beginning or origin. Derived from the longer phrase in Horace's Satire 1.3: "ab ovo usque ad mala", meaning "from the egg to the apples", referring to how Ancient Roman meals would typically begin with an egg dish and end with fruit (cf. the English phrase soup to nuts). Thus, ab ovo means "from the beginning", and can connote thoroughness.

znacenje.hr: Potječe iz Horacija; znači započeti priču ili izlaganje od samog početka.

common_translation.en: an absent person will not be an heir

entries.meaning: Legal principle that a person who is not present is unlikely to inherit.

znacenje.hr: Pravna maksima koja naglašava potrebu prisutnosti ili prihvaćanja nasljedstva.

entries.meaning: Legal phrase denoting action "in the absence of the accused".

znacenje.hr: Pravna oznaka za postupak vođen bez prisutnosti optužene osobe.

entries.meaning: i.e., "no offense", meaning to wish that no insult or injury be presumed or done by the speaker's words. Also rendered as absit iniuria verbis ("let injury be absent from these words"). cf. absit invidia.

znacenje.hr: Izraz kojim se naglašava da se ne želi nikoga uvrijediti.

entries.meaning: As opposed to "no offense", absit invidia is said in the context of a statement of excellence, to ward off envious deities who might interpret a statement of excellence as hubris. Also extended to absit invidia verbo ("may ill will/envy be absent from these words"). cf. absit iniuria verbis.

znacenje.hr: Izraz dobre volje i odricanja od zavisti.

entries.meaning: i.e., "let this not be a bad omen", expressing the hope that something ill-boding does not turn out to be bad luck in the future.

znacenje.hr: Izraz kojim se odbacuje loš predznak ili zla slutnja.

entries.meaning: i.e., total or supreme power, dominion, ownership, or sovereignty

znacenje.hr: Potpuno i neograničeno pravo vlasništva.

entries.meaning: Legal term pronounced by a judge in order to acquit a defendant following their trial. Te absolvo or absolvo te ("I forgive you") is said by Roman Catholic priests during the Sacrament of Confession, prior to the Second Vatican Council and in vernacular thereafter.

znacenje.hr: Pravna ili liturgijska formula oslobađanja od krivnje.

entries.meaning: i.e., "one can never be too careful"

znacenje.hr: Maksima koja opravdava dodatne mjere sigurnosti.

entries.meaning: Refers to situations in which a single example or observation indicates a general or universal truth. Coined in Virgil, Aeneid II 65-6. Example: visible in the court of King Silas in the American television series Kings.

znacenje.hr: Iz jedne osobe ili primjera zaključuje se o cijeloj skupini.

common_translation.en: from the founding of the City

entries.meaning: i.e., "from the founding of Rome", which occurred in 753 BC, according to Livy. It was used as a referential year in ancient Rome from which subsequent years were calculated, prior to being replaced by other dating conventions. Also anno urbis conditae (AUC), literally "in the year of the founded city".

znacenje.hr: datiranje godina po rimskom kalendaru, koje počinje s god. 753. pr. Kr., kada je Romul utemeljio Rim.

entries.meaning: The misuse of some thing does not eliminate the possibility of its correct use. cf. ab abusu ad usum non valet consequentia

znacenje.hr: Moralno-pravna maksima o razlici između stvari i njezine zlouporabe.

entries.meaning: Used of an argument

znacenje.hr: Argument koji se temelji na praktičnoj koristi.

entries.meaning: From Psalms 42:7; some translations have "sea calls to sea".

znacenje.hr: Biblijski izraz koji opisuje lanac nesreća ili duboku duhovnu krizu.

entries.meaning: Motto of the 848 Naval Air Squadron, British Royal Navy

znacenje.hr: Jednostavna formula davanja ili predaje.

entries.phrase: accusare nemo se debet nisi coram Deo
entries.normalized: accusare nemo se debet nisi coram deo
lemma: accusare nemo se debet nisi coram deo

common_translation.en: no one ought to accuse himself except in the presence of God
common_translation.hr: nitko se ne treba optuživati osim pred Bogom

entries.meaning: Legal principle denoting that an accused person is entitled to plead not guilty, and that a witness is not obligated to respond or submit a document that would incriminate himself. A similar phrase is nemo tenetur se ipsum accusare ("no one is bound to accuse himself").

znacenje.hr: Izraz koji naglašava savjest i božanski sud.

common_translation.en: mortal actions never deceive the gods
common_translation.hr: ljudska djela nikada ne varaju bogove

entries.meaning: Derived from Ovid, Tristia, I.ii, 97: si tamen acta deos numquam mortalia fallunt, / a culpa facinus scitis abesse mea. ("Yet if mortal actions never deceive the gods, / you know that crime was absent from my fault.")

znacenje.hr: Misao o božanskoj pravednosti i svevidnosti.

common_translation.en: The play has been performed; applaud!

entries.meaning: Common ending to ancient Roman comedies: Suetonius claimed in The Twelve Caesars that these were the last words of Augustus; Sibelius applied them to the third movement of his String Quartet No. 2, so that his audience would recognize that it was the last one, because a fourth would be ordinarily expected.
Motto of the United States Merchant Marine Academy.

znacenje.hr: Naglašava važnost djelovanja nad govorom.

entries.meaning: Also used in the singular preceding a saint's name: Acta Sancti ("Deeds of Saint") N.; a common title of hagiography works

znacenje.hr: Naziv zbirki životopisa i čudesa svetaca.

entries.meaning: i.e., "we act according to what we believe (ourselves to be)."

znacenje.hr: Teološki pojam koji povezuje vjeru i djela.

common_translation.en: A defendant is exonerated by the failure of the prosecution to prove its case
common_translation.hr: ako tužitelj ne dokaže, optuženi se oslobađa

entries.meaning: presumption of innocence

znacenje.hr: Temeljno pravilo kaznenog prava.

entries.phrase: actus me invito factus non est meus actus
entries.normalized: actus me invito factus non est meus actus
lemma: actus me invito factus non est meus actus

common_translation.en: the act done by me against my will is not my act
common_translation.hr: čin učinjen protiv moje volje nije moj čin

znacenje.hr: Naglašava važnost slobodne volje za odgovornost.

entries.phrase: actus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea
entries.normalized: actus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea
lemma: actus non facit reum nisi mens sit rea

common_translation.en: The act does not make [a person] guilty unless the mind should be guilty.
common_translation.hr: djelo ne čini krivim bez krive namjere

entries.meaning: Legal principle of the presumption of mens rea in a crime

znacenje.hr: Temeljna maksima kaznenog prava.

entries.meaning: The actual crime that is committed, as opposed to the intent, thinking, and rationalizing that procured the criminal act; the external elements of a crime, rather than the internal elements (i.e. mens rea).

znacenje.hr: Vanjski, objektivni element kaznenog djela.

entries.meaning: In logic, to the point of being silly or nonsensical. See also reductio ad absurdum. Not to be confused with ab absurdo ("from the absurd").

znacenje.hr: Latinski izraz koji se koristi u logici i retorici, osobito u argumentaciji (argumentum ad absurdum). Metoda seže do Aristotela (4. st. pr. Kr.), a znači dokazivanje teze tako da se suprotna tvrdnja dovede do besmislenog ili proturječnog zaključka.

entries.meaning: Used in legal language when providing additional evidence to an already sufficient collection. Also used commonly as an equivalent of "as if this wasn't enough".

znacenje.hr: Izraz znači „preko mjere, u preobilju“. U srednjovjekovnoj latinskoj stilistici koristio se za označavanje pretjerivanja; u hrvatskom odgovara izrazu „na pretek“.

entries.meaning: Denoting the irrelevance of a thing

znacenje.hr: Administrativni i pravni izraz rimskog podrijetla; znači da je predmet završen i pohranjen. U suvremenom hrvatskom: „arhivirano“.

entries.meaning: Motto of Happy Grove High School in Hector's River, Jamaica.

znacenje.hr: Humanistički moto, često korišten u vojnim i političkim kontekstima novog vijeka; naglašava stalnu pripravnost.
Moto renesansnog i prosvjetiteljskog duha; izražava težnju za višim ciljevima i moralnim ili intelektualnim uzdizanjem.
Pravnički izraz rimskog prava; označava slobodnu procjenu ili diskrecijsko pravo.

entries.meaning: A common name or motto, in whole or part, among many publications

znacenje.hr: Pjesnički izraz antičkog podrijetla; simbolizira težnju prema slavi ili besmrtnosti.

entries.meaning: i.e., "a rough road leads to the stars", as on the Launch Complex 34 memorial plaque for the astronauts of Apollo 1. Used as a motto by the State of Kansas and other organisations

znacenje.hr: Poznata maksima iz rimske književnosti (Seneka, 1. st.); znači da se uspjeh postiže kroz poteškoće.

entries.meaning: i.e., to rise to a high position overcoming hardships.

znacenje.hr: Varijanta prethodne maksime; često korištena kao plemićki ili vojni moto u ranom novom vijeku.

entries.meaning: i.e., to appeal to the masses. Often said of or used by politicians. Likewise, an argumentum ad captandum is an argument designed to please the crowd.

znacenje.hr: Retorički izraz; označava postupke kojima se nastoji pridobiti neuka publika, često laskavim ili površnim sredstvima.

entries.meaning: Formal letter or communication in the Christian tradition from a bishop to his clergy. An ad clerum may be an encouragement in a time of celebration or a technical explanation of new regulations or canons.

znacenje.hr: Izraz korišten u crkvenim dokumentima; odnosi se na obraćanje ili djelovanje usmjereno prema svećenstvu.

entries.phrase: ad coelum ora caelo usque ad centrum
entries.normalized: ad coelum ora caelo usque ad centrum
lemma: ad coelum ora caelo usque ad centrum

common_translation.en: from the sky to the center

entries.meaning: i.e., "from Heaven all the way to the center of the Earth". In law, it may refer to the proprietary principle of cuius est solum, eius est usque ad coelum et ad inferos ("whosesoever is the soil, it is his up to the sky and down to the depths [of the Earth]").

znacenje.hr: Rijetka, kozmološka fraza; odražava antičko i srednjovjekovno shvaćanje svemira kao hijerarhijski uređenog.

entries.meaning: An ad eundem degree (derived from ad eundem gradum, "to the same step or degree") is a courtesy degree awarded by a university or college to an alumnus of another. Rather than an honorary degree, it is a recognition of the formal learning for which the degree was earned at another college.

znacenje.hr: Bibliografska oznaka u znanstvenim radovima; znači „na isto mjesto“ ili „istom autoru“.

entries.meaning: Motto of Renaissance humanism and the Protestant Reformation

znacenje.hr: Humanistički moto renesanse (15.–16. st.), osobito Erazma Rotterdamskog; poziv na povratak izvornim tekstovima.

entries.meaning: i.e., "bottoms up!" (during a generic toast) or "back to the basics", depending on context.

znacenje.hr: Kolokvijalni latinski izraz; u hrvatskom odgovara „do kraja“.

entries.meaning: i.e., "for this", in the sense of improvised or intended only for a specific, immediate purpose.

znacenje.hr: Izraz rimskog prava; označava privremeno rješenje. U suvremenom jeziku često ima blago pejorativno značenje.

entries.meaning: Provides the term argumentum ad hominem, a logical fallacy in which a person themselves is criticized, when the subject of debate is their idea or argument, on the mistaken assumption that the soundness of an argument is dependent on the qualities of the proponent.

znacenje.hr: Logički i retorički pojam iz Aristotelove tradicije; označava napad na osobu umjesto na argument.

entries.meaning: i.e., not for the purpose of gaining any material reward

znacenje.hr: Akademski izraz; rad ili dužnost obavlja se bez novčane naknade, radi ugleda.

entries.meaning: i.e., enduring forever. Used to designate a property which repeats in all cases in mathematical proof. Also used in philosophical contexts to mean "repeating in all cases".

znacenje.hr: Filozofski izraz; koristi se od antike za označavanje nečega bez kraja.

entries.meaning: As in the term "chargé d'affaires ad interim", denoting a diplomatic officer who acts in place of an ambassador.

znacenje.hr: Administrativni izraz; znači „u međuvremenu“, dok se ne donese konačna odluka.

entries.meaning: i.e., "when pigs fly". Attributed by Suetonius in The Twelve Caesars to Augustus. The Calends were specific days of the Roman calendar, not of the Greek, and so the "Greek Kalends" would never occur.

znacenje.hr: Rimski izraz; Grci nisu imali kalende, pa fraza znači da se nešto neće dogoditi.

entries.meaning: i.e, "according to what pleases" or "as you wish". In music and theatrical scripts, it typically indicates that the performer has the liberty to change or omit something. Ad lib is often, specifically used when one improvises or ignores limitations. Also used by some restaurants in favor of the colloquial "all you can eat or drink". Libitum comes from the past participle of libere ("to please").

common_translation.en: to the thresholds of the Apostles

entries.meaning: i.e., to Rome. Refers specifically to the quinquennial visit ad limina, a formal trip by Roman Catholic bishops to visit the Pope every five years.

znacenje.hr: Crkveni izraz; označava obvezni posjet biskupa Rimu, grobovima sv. Petra i Pavla.

entries.meaning: Legal phrase referring to a party appointed by a court to act in a lawsuit on behalf of another party who is deemed incapable of representing himself or herself, such as a child. An individual who acts in this capacity is called a guardian ad litem.

znacenje.hr: Pravnički izraz rimskog prava; odnosi se na sudski spor.

entries.meaning: Used to suggest looking for information about a term in the corresponding place in a cited work of reference.

znacenje.hr: Znanstvena i komentatorska oznaka u tekstovima.

entries.meaning: frequently used motto for educational institutions

znacenje.hr: Metaforički izraz; znači iznijeti nešto na vidjelo, poznat u kršćanskoj literaturi.

common_translation.en: For the greater glory of God

entries.meaning: motto of the Society of Jesus (Jesuits)

znacenje.hr: Isusovački moto, povezan sa sv. Ignacijem Loyolom (16. st.).

entries.meaning: Motto of St Patrick's College, Cavan, Ireland

znacenje.hr: Optimistična maksima; česta u humanističkim i modernim kontekstima.

entries.meaning: Medical phrase serving as a synonym for death

znacenje.hr: Pravnički i književni izraz; označava trajanje do kraja života.

entries.meaning: Wish for a long life; similar to "many happy returns".

znacenje.hr: Liturgijski i svečani izraz; koristi se kao čestitka ili aklamacija.

entries.meaning: i.e., "to the point of disgust". Sometimes used as a humorous alternative to ad infinitum. An argumentum ad nauseam is a logical fallacy in which erroneous proof is proffered by prolonged repetition of the argument, i. e., the argument is repeated so many times that persons are "sick of it".

znacenje.hr: Retorički izraz; znači pretjerano ponavljanje.

entries.meaning: i.e., "obvious on sight" or "obvious to anyone that sees it"

znacenje.hr: Izraz koji naglašava neposrednost i očitost.

common_translation.en: to the foot of the letter

entries.meaning: i.e., "exactly as it is written", "to the letter", or "to the very last detail"

znacenje.hr: Filološki izraz; znači „slijedeći slovo (teksta)“.

entries.meaning: Generally precedes "of" and a person's name, used to wish for someone to be remembered long after death

znacenje.hr: Crkveno-pravni izraz; koristi se u službenim dokumentima.

common_translation.en: to the weight of all things

entries.meaning: i.e., "considering everything's weight". The abbreviation was historically used by physicians and others to signify that the last prescribed ingredient is to weigh as much as all of the previously mentioned ones.

i.e., "according to the harm" or "in proportion to the harm". The phrase is used in tort law as a measure of damages inflicted, implying that a remedy (if one exists) ought to correspond specifically and only to the damage suffered. cf. damnum absque iniuria.

znacenje.hr: Pravnički izraz; koristi se u odštetnim postupcima.

entries.meaning: i.e., subject to be proposed, provisionally approved, but still needing official approval. Not the same as a referendum.
i.e., "to the point" or "without digression"

znacenje.hr: Retorički izraz; znači govoriti bitno i relevantno.

entries.meaning: Patch motto of the US Naval Station Norfolk

znacenje.hr: Vojni izraz antičkog podrijetla; naglašava borbenu spremnost.

entries.meaning: Motto of the Brazilian Marine Corps. A prayer Adsumus, Sancte Spiritus (We stand before You, Holy Spirit) is typically said at the start of every session of an Ecumenical Council or Synod of Bishops in the Catholic Church.

znacenje.hr: Svečani izraz; koristi se u liturgiji i formalnim okupljanjima.

common_translation.en: in order to achieve what has been undertaken

entries.meaning: Motto of the Association of Trust Schools

znacenje.hr: Pravničko-moralna maksima rimskog podrijetla.

common_translation.en: for the term which has passed

entries.meaning: The word aetatis means "of age" (e.g. "aetatis 36" denotes being "of year of age 36" or "in the 36th year of age". Because the first year of age is entered at birth, this is equivalent to "aged 35 years old".) Appears on portraits, gravestones, monuments, etc. Usually preceded by anno (AAS), "in the year # [of his age/life]". Frequently combined with Anno Domini, giving a date as both the age of Jesus Christ and the age of the decedent. Example: "Obiit anno Domini MDCXXXVIo (tricensimo sexto), [anno] aetatis suae XXVo (vicensimo quinto)" ("he died in the 1636th year of the Lord, [being] the 25th [year] of his age[/life]").
Legal term derived from fides ("faith"), originating at least from Medieval Latin to denote a statement under oath.

znacenje.hr: Pravnički pojam iz anglosaksonskog prava; označava pisanu izjavu danu pod prisegom pred ovlaštenom osobom. Razvio se u engleskom pravu u kasnom srednjem vijeku; u hrvatskom pravu nema izravnog pandana, ali odgovara „izjavi pod prisegom“.

entries.meaning: i.e., "do what you are doing," or "do well whatever you do." Figuratively, it means "keep going, because you are inspired or dedicated to do so." This is the motto of several Roman Catholic schools, and was also used by Pope John XXIII in the sense of "do not be concerned with any other matter than the task in hand;" he was allaying worry of what would become of him in the future: his sense of age quod agis was "joy" regarding what is presently occurring and "detachment" from concern of the future.

entries.meaning: Metaphysical and moral principle that indicates the connection of ontology, obligation, and ethics.

Refers both to the innocence of a lamb and to Christ being a sacrificial lamb after the Jewish religious practice. It is the Latin translation from John 1:36, when St. John the Baptist exclaimes "Ecce Agnus Dei!" ("Behold the Lamb of God!") upon seeing Jesus Christ.

The original meaning was similar to "the game is afoot", but its modern meaning, like that of the phrase "crossing the Rubicon", denotes passing the point of no return on a momentous decision and entering into a risky endeavor where the outcome is left to chance.

entries.meaning: Abbreviation of Anno Domini Nostri Jesu Christi ("in the year of Our Lord Jesus Christ"), the predominantly-used system for dating years across the world; used with the Gregorian Calendar and based on the perceived year of the birth of Jesus Christ. The years before His birth were formerly signified by a. C. n (ante Christum natum, "before Christ was born"), but now use the English abbreviation "BC" ("before Christ"). For example, Augustus was born in the year 63 BC and died in AD 14.

Variation on annus mirabilis, recorded in print from 1890. Notably used in a speech by Queen Elizabeth II to describe what a bad year 1992 had been for her. In Classical Latin, this phrase actually means "terrifying year". See also annus terribilis.

entries.meaning: Used particularly to refer to the years 1665 and 1666, during which Isaac Newton made revolutionary inventions and discoveries in calculus, motion, optics and gravitation. Annus Mirabilis is also the title of a poem by John Dryden written in the same year. It has since been used to refer to other years, especially to 1905, when Albert Einstein made equally revolutionary discoveries concerning the photoelectric effect, Brownian motion, mass-energy equivalence, and the special theory of relativity. (See Annus Mirabilis papers) another use was the Annus Mirabilis of 1759 to commemorate the string of victories won by Britain and her allies.

znacenje.hr: Izraz za iznimno plodnu godinu; poznat po Newtonu (1666.).

entries.meaning: Or "reasoning", "inference", "appeal", or "proof". The plural is argumenta. Commonly used in the names of logical arguments and fallacies, preceding phrases such as a silentio (by silence), ad antiquitatem (to antiquity), ad baculum (to the stick), ad captandum (to capturing), ad consequentiam (to the consequence), ad crumenam (to the purse), ad feminam (to the woman), ad hominem (to the person), ad ignorantiam (to ignorance), ad invidiam (to envy/jealousy/odium/hatred/reproach – appealing to low passions), ad judicium (to judgment), ad lazarum (to poverty), ad logicam (to logic), ad metum (to fear), ad misericordiam (to pity), ad nauseam (to nausea), ad novitatem (to novelty), ad personam (to the character), ad numerum (to the number), ad odium (to spite), ad populum (to the people), ad temperantiam (to moderation), ad verecundiam (to reverence), ex silentio (from silence), in terrorem (into terror), and e contrario (from/to the opposite).

entries.meaning: From Virgil, Aeneid, Book 10, 284, where the first word is in an archaic form, audentis fortuna iuvat. Also the motto of the Portuguese Army Commandos and the USS Montpelier in the latter form.

Charlie Chaplin also used the phrase in The Great Dictator to ridicule Hynkel's (Chaplin's parody of Hitler) ambition for power, but substituted "nullus" for "nihil".

entries.meaning: Or, "do or die" or "no retreat". A Greek expression («Ἢ τὰν ἢ ἐπὶ τᾶς») that Spartan mothers said to their sons as they departed for battle. It refers to the practices that a Greek hoplite would drop his cumbersome shield in order to flee the battlefield, and a slain warrior would be borne home atop his shield.

Later versions included a variant of "We who are about to die", and this translation is sometimes aided by changing the Latin to nos morituri te salutamus.

entries.meaning: John of Cornwall (ca. 1170) was once asked by a scribe what the word meant. It turns out that the original text said in diebus illis [in those days], which the scribe misread as in die busillis [at the day of Busillis], believing this was a famous man. This mondegreen has since entered the literature; it occurs in Alessandro Manzoni's novel The Betrothed (1827), in Dostoevsky's The Brothers Karamazov (1880), and in Andrea Camilleri's Inspector Montalbano series.

znacenje.hr: Talijanizam iz krivo shvaćenog latinskog; znači lažni problem.

entries.meaning: From Gottfried August Bürger's Prinzessin Europa (line 60); popularised by Heinrich Heine's Deutschland. Ein Wintermärchen (XI, 44); also the title of Joseph Haydn's canon for four voices, Hob. XXVIIb:16; Ludwig van Beethoven set the text by Bürger as a three-voice canon, WoO 224. Contemporary critics applied this epithet to both of Turner's Regulus (1828 and 1837).

Coram vobis is the analogous version ("let the matters remain before you") for the Court of Common Pleas, where the King did not sit, even notionally.

entries.meaning: A concept about creation, often used in a theological or philosophical context. Also known as the 'First Cause' argument in philosophy of religion. Contrasted with creatio ex materia.

znacenje.hr: Teološki pojam; biblijska kozmologija.

entries.meaning: The first words of the Nicene Creed and the Apostles' Creed.

znacenje.hr: Početak Nicejskog vjerovanja.

common_translation.en: I believe it because it is absurd

entries.meaning: A very common misquote of Tertullian's et mortuus est Dei Filius prorsus credibile quia ineptum est (and the Son of God is dead: in short, it is credible because it is unfitting), meaning that it is so absurd to say that God's son has died that it would have to be a matter of belief, rather than reason. The misquoted phrase, however, is commonly used to mock the dogmatic beliefs of the religious (see fideism). This phrase is commonly shortened to credo quia absurdum, and is also sometimes rendered credo quia impossibile est (I believe it because it is impossible) or, as Darwin used it in his autobiography, credo quia incredibile.

znacenje.hr: Pripisuje se Tertulijanu; paradoks vjere.

common_translation.en: I believe so that I may understand

entries.meaning: A motto of St Anselm, used as the motto of St. Anselm Hall, Manchester

znacenje.hr: Augustin i Anzelmo; vjera prethodi razumu.

common_translation.en: May we grow in Him through all things

entries.meaning: Motto of Cheverus High School

znacenje.hr: Poslanica Efežanima; duhovni rast.

common_translation.en: let knowledge grow, let life be enriched
common_translation.hr: neka raste znanje, neka se njeguje život

entries.meaning: Motto of the University of Chicago; often rendered in English as an iambic tetrameter, "Let knowledge grow from more to more, and so be human life enriched".

znacenje.hr: Moto Sveučilišta u Chicagu; prosvjetiteljski ideal.

entries.meaning: Motto of James Cook University

znacenje.hr: Metaforički izraz; napredak i razjašnjenje.

entries.meaning: Motto of Maryland until 1874

znacenje.hr: Knjiga Postanka; biblijska zapovijed.

entries.meaning: Motto of Claremont McKenna College.

From Lucretius' De rerum natura book VI, where it refers in context to the motion of a thunderbolt across the sky, which acquires power and momentum as it goes. This metaphor was adapted as the state motto of New Mexico (adopted in 1887 as the territory's motto, and kept in 1912 when New Mexico received statehood) and is seen on the seal. Also the motto of Rocky Mount, Virginia and Omega Delta Phi.

common_translation.en: while I live, I trust in the cross, Whilst I trust in the Cross I have life

entries.meaning: Motto of the Sisters of Loreto (IBVM) and its associated schools.

common_translation.en: The hood does not make the monk

entries.meaning: William Shakespeare, Twelfth Night, act 1, scene 5, 53–54

"Who benefits?" An adage in criminal investigation which suggests that considering who would benefit from an unwelcome event is likely to reveal who is responsible for that event (cf. cui prodest). Also the motto of the Crime Syndicate of America, a fictional supervillain group. The opposite is cui malo (Bad for whom?).

entries.phrase: cui multum sit datum, multum ab eo postulabitur
entries.normalized: cui multum sit datum, multum ab eo postulabitur
lemma: cui multum sit datum, multum ab eo postulabitur

common_translation.en: to whom much is given, much is expected

entries.meaning: Motto of The Brooklyn Latin School.
Short for cui prodest scelus is fecit (for whom the crime advances, he has done it) in Seneca's Medea. Thus, the murderer is often the one who gains by the murder (cf. cui bono).

entries.phrase: cuius est solum, eius est usque ad coelum et ad inferos
entries.normalized: cuius est solum, eius est usque ad coelum et ad inferos
lemma: cuius est solum, eius est usque ad coelum et ad inferos

common_translation.en: Whose the land is, all the way to the sky and to the underworld is his.

entries.meaning: First coined by Accursius of Bologna in the 13th century. A Roman legal principle of property law that is no longer observed in most situations today. Less literally, "For whosoever owns the soil, it is theirs up to the sky and down to the depths."
The privilege of a ruler to choose the religion of his subjects. A regional prince's ability to choose his people's religion was established at the Peace of Augsburg in 1555.

entries.phrase: cuiusvis hominis est errare, nullius nisi insipientis in errore perseverare.
entries.normalized: cuiusvis hominis est errare, nullius nisi insipientis in errore perseverare.
lemma: cuiusvis hominis est errare, nullius nisi insipientis in errore perseverare.

common_translation.en: Anyone can err, but only the fool persists in his fault

entries.meaning: Cicero, Philippica XII, 5.
Also "blame" or "guilt". In law, an act of neglect. In general, guilt, sin, or a fault. See also mea culpa.

entries.phrase: Cum Deo pro Patria et Libertate
entries.normalized: cum deo pro patria et libertate
lemma: cum deo pro patria et libertate

common_translation.en: With God for Fatherland and Liberty

entries.meaning: Appears on Francis II Rákóczi's flag
From the Bible. Occurs in Matthew 26:47 and Luke 22:52.
Motto of a well-paid soldier. See salary.
Not to be taken too seriously or as the literal truth.

common_translation.en: with this, therefore on account of this

entries.meaning: Fallacy of assuming that correlation implies causation.
The standard formula for academic Latin honors in the United States. Greater honors include magna cum laude and summa cum laude.

common_translation.en: with the dead in a dead language

entries.meaning: Movement from Pictures at an Exhibition by Modest Mussorgsky

common_translation.en: with the exclusive right to print

entries.meaning: Copyright notice used in 16th-century England, used for comic effect in The Taming of the Shrew by William Shakespeare where Lucentio is urged by his servant Biondello to "seize your privilege to declare her [Bianca] yours alone".

entries.phrase: cuncti adsint meritaeque expectent praemia palmae
entries.normalized: cuncti adsint meritaeque expectent praemia palmae
lemma: cuncti adsint meritaeque expectent praemia palmae

common_translation.en: let all come who by merit deserve the most reward

entries.meaning: Motto of University College London.
From the Bible, locution indicating a will to death ("I want to die").

The question attributed to Anselm in his work of by this name, wherein he reflects on why the Christ of Christianity must be both fully Divine and fully Human. Often translated "why did God become Man?"
Motto of Georgetown University School of Medicine and University of Scranton

common_translation.en: take care of your own self

entries.meaning: Exhortation to physicians, or experts in general, to deal with their own problems before addressing those of others
An overview of a person's life and qualifications, similar to a résumé
Motto of the City of Westminster
A censor
Motto of Western Australia
A traditional greeting of Czech brewers.

entries.phrase: da mihi factum, dabo tibi ius
entries.normalized: da mihi factum, dabo tibi ius
lemma: da mihi factum, dabo tibi ius

common_translation.en: Give me the fact, I will give you the law

entries.meaning: Also da mihi facta, dabo tibi ius (plural "facta" (facts) for the singular "factum"). A legal principle of Roman law that parties to a suit should present the facts and the judge will rule on the law that governs them. Related to iura novit curia (the court knows the law).

common_translation.en: They condemn what they do not understand

entries.meaning: Paraphrase of Quintilianus, De Institutione Oratoria, Book 10, chapter 1, 26: "Modesto tamen et circumspecto iudicio de tantis viris pronuntiandum est, ne, quod plerisque accidit, damnent quae non intellegunt." [Yet students must pronounce with diffidence and circumspection on the merits of such illustrious characters, lest, as is the case with many, they condemn what they do not understand. (translated by Rev. John Selby Watson)
Colloquially, "thrown to the lions".
The ancient Roman custom by which it was pretended that disgraced Romans, especially former emperors, never existed, by eliminating all records and likenesses of them.

Meaning a loss that results from no one's wrongdoing. In Roman law, a person is not responsible for unintended, consequential injury to another that results from a lawful act. This protection does not necessarily apply to unintended damage caused by one's negligence or folly.

entries.phrase: dat deus incrementum, or, deus dat incrementum
entries.normalized: dat deus incrementum, or, deus dat incrementum
lemma: dat deus incrementum, or, deus dat incrementum
entries.meaning: Motto of several schools.

common_translation.en: with due respect / given the excuse

entries.meaning: Used before disagreeing with someone.

common_translation.en: We shall accomplish the mission assigned

entries.meaning: Motto of Batalhão de Operações Policiais Especiais (BOPE), Rio de Janeiro, Brazil.
In law, a de bene esse deposition is used to preserve the testimony of a witness who is expected not to be available to appear at trial and be cross-examined.
In law, trespass de bonis asportatis was the traditional name for larceny, i.e., the unlawful theft of chattels (moveable goods).
Used, e.g., in "as we agreed in the meeting d.d. 26th May 2006".

Said of something that is the actual state of affairs, in contrast to something's legal or official standing, which is described as de jure. De facto refers to "the way things really are" rather than what is officially presented as the fact of the matter in question.
A clerk of a court makes this declaration when he is appointed, by which he promises to perform his duties faithfully as a servant of the court.
Describes an oath taken to faithfully administer the duties of a job or office, like that taken by a court reporter.
Usually used in the context of "at a future time".

common_translation.en: Of tastes there is nothing to be disputed

entries.meaning: Less literally, "there is no accounting for taste", because they are judged subjectively and not objectively: everyone has their own and none deserve preeminence. The complete phrase is "de gustibus et coloribus non est disputandum" ("when we talk about tastes and colours there is nothing to be disputed"). Probably of Scholastic origin; see Wiktionary.
"Official", in contrast with de facto; analogous to "in principle", whereas de facto is to "in practice". In other contexts, it can mean "according to law", "by right", and "legally".

common_translation.en: of/from law passed / of/from law in force

entries.meaning: from [a person's] language [group]; party jury; the right to a jury disproportionally chosen from the accused's ethnic group; see struck jury.

common_translation.en: The law does not care about the smallest things.

entries.meaning: A court does not care about small, trivial things. A case must have some importance in order for a court to hear it. See "de minimis non curat praetor". Also used as an adjective: "The court found that the alleged conduct was de minimis."

common_translation.en: The commander does not care about the smallest things.

entries.meaning: Also, "The chief magistrate does not concern himself with trifles." Trivial matters are no concern of a high official; cf. aquila non capit muscas (the eagle does not catch flies). Sometimes rex (king) or lex (law) is used in place of praetor.

entries.phrase: de mortuis aut bene aut nihil
entries.normalized: de mortuis aut bene aut nihil
lemma: de mortuis aut bene aut nihil

common_translation.en: about the dead, either well or nothing

entries.meaning: Less literally, "speak well of the dead or not at all"; cf. de mortuis nil nisi bonum.

common_translation.en: about the dead, nothing unless a good thing

entries.meaning: From de mortuis nil nisi bonum dicendum est ("nothing must be said about the dead except the good"), attributed by Diogenes Laërtius to Chilon. In legal contexts, this quotation is used with the opposite meaning: defamation of a deceased person is not a crime. In other contexts, it refers to taboos against criticizing the recently deceased.

common_translation.en: About us is the story told

entries.meaning: Thus: "their story is our story". Originally it referred to the end of Rome's dominance. Now often used when comparing any current situation to a past story or event.

"Anew" or "afresh". In law, a trial de novo is a retrial of the issues as though they had not been tried before. In biology, de novo means newly synthesized, and a de novo mutation is a mutation that neither parent possessed or transmitted. In economics, de novo refers to newly founded companies, and de novo banks are state banks that have been in operation for five years or less. (Cf. ex novo)

entries.phrase: de omni re scibili et quibusdam aliis
entries.normalized: de omni re scibili et quibusdam aliis
lemma: de omni re scibili et quibusdam aliis

common_translation.en: about every knowable thing, and even certain other things

entries.meaning: The Italian scholar Giovanni Pico della Mirandola of the 15th century wrote the De omni re scibili ("concerning every knowable thing") part, and a wag added et quibusdam aliis ("and even certain other things").

common_translation.en: Be suspicious of everything / doubt everything

entries.meaning: Attributed to the French philosopher René Descartes. It was also Karl Marx's favorite motto and a title of one of Søren Kierkegaard's works, namely, De Omnibus Dubitandum Est.
Loosely, "to liberate the oppressed". Motto of the United States Army Special Forces.
Motto of the Worshipful Company of Barbers.
Meaning from out of the depths of misery or dejection. From the Latin translation of the Vulgate Bible of Psalm 130, of which it is a traditional title in Roman Catholic liturgy.
entries.author: Vulgata

entries.meaning: In logic, de dicto statements regarding the truth of a proposition are distinguished from de re statements regarding the properties of a thing itself.
Used in genealogical records, often abbreviated as d.s.p., to indicate a person who died without having had any children.
Used in genealogical records, often abbreviated as d.s.p.l., to indicate a person who died without having had any children with a spouse.

Used in genealogical records in cases of nobility or other hereditary titles, often abbreviated as d.s.p.m.l. or d.s.p.m. legit, to indicate a person who died without having had any legitimate male children (indicating there were illegitimate male children)
Used in genealogical records, often abbreviated as d.s.p.m., to indicate a person who died without having had any male children who survived, i.e. outlived him.
Used in genealogical records, often abbreviated as d.s.p.s., to indicate a person who died without having had any children who survived, i.e. outlived him.

common_translation.en: died in the lifetime of the mother

entries.meaning: Used in genealogical records, often abbreviated as d.v.m., to indicate a person who predeceased his or her mother.

common_translation.en: died in the lifetime of the father

entries.meaning: Used in genealogical records, often abbreviated as d.v.p., to indicate a person who predeceased his or her father.

A phrase from Virgil's Aeneid. Inscription on British one-pound coins. Originally inscribed on coins of the 17th century, it refers to the inscribed edge of the coin as a protection against the clipping of its precious metal.
Official motto of the United States Air Force Security Forces (Security Police).
Part of the full style of a monarch historically considered to be ruling by divine right, notably in the style of the English and British monarch since 1521

common_translation.en: By the Grace of God, King

entries.meaning: Also Dei gratia regina ("By the Grace of God, Queen"). Abbreviated as D G REX on British and Canadian coins. Also occurs on coins of the Holy Roman Empire such as the Otto Adelheid Pfennig.
Motto of Princeton University, Princeton, New Jersey, United States.

In Catholic theology, pleasure taken in a sinful thought or imagination, such as brooding on sexual images. As voluntary and complacent erotic fantasizing, without attempt to suppress such thoughts, it is distinct from actual sexual desire.

common_translation.en: Delegated powers can not be [further] delegated

entries.meaning: A legal principle whereby one to whom certain powers were delegated may not ipso facto re-delegate them to another. A distinction may be had between delegated powers and the additional power to re-delegate them.
A Latin translation of René Goscinny's phrase in French ils sont fous, ces romains! or Italian Sono pazzi questi Romani. Cf. SPQR, which Obelix frequently used in the Asterix comics.
Motto of Colgate University.
Motto of Somerset College.
Motto of Charterhouse School.
Motto of Methodist Ladies' College, Melbourne.
Motto of Regis High School in New York City, New York, United States.
A frequent phrase in the Roman Catholic liturgy, used especially after the recitation of a lesson, the Last Gospel at Mass or as a response to Ite Missa Est / Benedicamus Domino.
Motto of Monaco and its monarch, which is inscribed on the royal arms.
Motto of the Epsom College in Surrey, England and Fairham Freemasons Lodge No.8002 in the province of Nottinghamshire.

common_translation.en: To the best and greatest God

entries.meaning: Derived from the pagan Iupiter optimo maximo ("to the best and greatest Jupiter"). Printed on bottles of Bénédictine liqueur.
motto of Scotch College (Melbourne)
motto of Bromsgrove School

common_translation.en: with God as protector / with an avenging God

entries.meaning: motto of the defunct Confederate States of America

This was often used in conjunction with a signature at the end of letters. It was used in order to signify that "God willing" this letter will get to you safely, "God willing" the contents of this letter come true. As an abbreviation (simply "D.V.") it is often found in personal letters (in English) of the early 1900s, employed to generally and piously qualify a given statement about a future planned action, that it will be carried out, so long as God wills it (see James 4:13–15, which encourages this way of speaking); cf. inshallah. Motto of Southern Illinois University Carbondale.

common_translation.en: The descent into the cave of the rabbit

entries.meaning: Down the rabbit hole; backtranslation, not a genuine Latin phrase; see Down the rabbit hole.
From Hebrews 11:16; the motto of the Order of Canada.
Title and first words of the first encyclical of Pope Benedict XVI. For other meanings see Deus caritas est (disambiguation).

From the Greek ἀπὸ μηχανῆς θεός (apò mēchanēs theós). A contrived or artificial solution, usually to a literary plot. Refers to the practice in Greek drama of lowering by crane (the mechanê) an actor playing a god or goddess onto the stage to resolve an insuperable conflict in the plot. The device is most commonly associated with Euripides.
The motto of The Catholic University of America.
The principal motto of Scottish Rite Freemasonry. See also Dieu et mon droit.

common_translation.en: God has given us these days of leisure

entries.meaning: Motto of the city of Liverpool, England.
Motto of Methodist College Belfast

common_translation.en: Get results, whether God likes it or not

entries.meaning: Literally: Results, God unwilling. Can also be rendered as "Deus Nolens Exituus".
The motto of Sir Thomas de Boteler, founder of Boteler Grammar School in Warrington in 1526.
The principal slogan of the Crusades. Motto of Bergen Catholic High School in New Jersey, United States.
Attributed to Fray Luis de León, the beginning of his first lecture after resuming his professorship at Salamanca University following four years of imprisonment by the Inquisition
A recent academic substitution for the spacious and inconvenient phrase "as previously stated". Literally, has been stated. Compare also "dicta prius"; literally, said previously.

I.e. "from a rule without exception." Short for a dicto simpliciter, the a is often dropped because it is confused with the English indefinite article. A dicto simpliciter occurs when an acceptable exception is ignored or eliminated. For example, the appropriateness of using opiates is contingent on suffering extreme pain. To justify the recreational use of opiates by referring to a cancer patient or to justify arresting said patient by comparing him to the recreational user would be a dicto simpliciter.
Motto of United States Navy Fighter Squadron VF-194.
Motto of the London Stock Exchange.
From the Roman Emperor Titus. Recorded in the biography of him by Suetonius in Lives of the Twelve Caesars.
Reference to the Judgment Day in Christian eschatology. The title of a famous Medieval Latin hymn by Tommaso da Celano in the 13th century and used in the Requiem Mass.

Days under common law (traditionally Sunday), during which no legal process can be served and any legal judgment is invalid. The English Parliament first codified this precept in the reign of King Charles II.

common_translation.en: a day as dark as night

entries.meaning: First entry in Annales Cambriae, for the year 447.

common_translation.en: He has half the deed done, who has made a beginning.

entries.meaning: From the second letter by Horace in his First Book of Letters: Dimidium facti, qui coepit, habet; sapere aude, incipe. [... dare to know, begin].
In Classical Latin, "I arrange". Motto of the State of Maine, United States; based on a comparison of the State to the star Polaris.

common_translation.en: It seemed otherwise to the gods

entries.meaning: In other words, the gods have ideas different from those of mortals, and so events do not always occur in the way persons wish them to. Cf. Virgil, Aeneid, 2: 428. Also cf. "Man proposes and God disposes" and "My Thoughts are not your thoughts, neither are your ways My ways", Isaiah 55, 8–9.

Refers to the Manes, i.e. Roman spirits of the dead. Loosely, "to the memory of". A conventional pagan inscription preceding the name of the deceased on their tombstone; often shortened to dis manibus (D.M.), "for the ghost-gods". Preceded in some earlier monuments by hic situs est (H. S. E.), "he lies here".

common_translation.en: learn or depart / learn or leave

entries.meaning: Motto of Royal College, Colombo and of King's School, Rochester.

entries.phrase: disce ut semper victurus, vive ut cras moriturus
entries.normalized: disce ut semper victurus, vive ut cras moriturus
lemma: disce ut semper victurus, vive ut cras moriturus

common_translation.en: Learn as if [you will] live forever; live as if [you will] die tomorrow.

entries.meaning: Attributed to St. Edmund of Abingdon; first seen in Isidoro de Sevilla
See also § docendo discitur
Motto of the three California Polytechnic State Universities of San Luis Obispo, Pomona, and Humboldt, United States.
I.e., "scattered remains". Paraphrased from Horace, Satires, 1, 4, 62, where it is written "disiecti membra poetae" (limbs of a scattered poet).
Motto of the State of Arizona, United States, adopted in 1911. Probably derived from the translation of the Vulgate Bible of Genesis 14: 23.
A Roman maxim adopted by Roman Dictator Julius Caesar, King Louis XI of France and the Italian political author Niccolò Machiavelli.
A popular, eloquent expression, usually used in the end of a speech. The implied meaning is that the speaker has said all that had to be said and thus the argument is completed.
Used to attribute a statement or opinion to its author, rather than the speaker.

common_translation.en: I give that you may give

entries.meaning: Often said or written of sacrifices, in which one "gives" and expects a return from the gods.

common_translation.en: It is learned by teaching / one learns by teaching

entries.meaning: Attributed to Seneca the Younger.

common_translation.en: I learn by teaching, I think by writing

entries.meaning: person's ability to commit a wrongful act with the knowledge that it is wrong; cf. doli incapax.

"The ... concept is particular to a few civil law systems and cannot sweepingly be equated with the notions of 'special' or 'specific intent' in common law systems. Of course, the same might equally be said of the concept of 'specific intent', a notion used in the common law almost exclusively within the context of the defense of voluntary intoxication." (Genocide scholar William A. Schabas)
Motto of the City of London, England.
Psalm 20, 10.
After Psalm 20, 10.

common_translation.en: Sunday in [Setting Aside the] White Garments

entries.meaning: Latin name of the Octave of Easter in the Roman Catholic liturgy.
Motto of the Southland College, Philippines. Psalm 28, 8.
Motto of the University of Oxford, England. Psalm 27, 1.
Motto of St. John's College and Prep School, Harare, Zimbabwe. After Psalm 23, 1.
A phrase used in the Roman Catholic liturgy, and sometimes in its sermons and homilies, and a general form of greeting among and towards members of Catholic organizations. See also Pax vobiscum.
Often set to music, either by itself or as the final phrase of the Agnus Dei prayer of the Holy Mass.

common_translation.en: a donation in expectation of death

entries.meaning: A legal concept in which a person in imminent mortal danger need not satisfy the otherwise requisite consideration to effect a testamentary donation, i.e., a donation by instituting or modifying a will.

common_translation.en: a sleeping dragon is never to be tickled

entries.meaning: Motto of the fictional Hogwarts School of Witchcraft and Wizardry of the Harry Potter series; translated more loosely in the books as "never tickle a sleeping dragon".
More literally, "the masks of the drama"; the cast of characters of a dramatic work.

entries.phrase: duae tabulae rasae in quibus nihil scriptum est
entries.normalized: duae tabulae rasae in quibus nihil scriptum est
lemma: duae tabulae rasae in quibus nihil scriptum est

common_translation.en: two blank slates with nothing written upon them

entries.meaning: Stan Laurel, inscription for the fan club logo of The Sons of the Desert.
Motto of the Royal Canadian Infantry Corps.
Motto of the 51st Battalion, Far North Queensland Regiment, Australia.

common_translation.en: the fates lead the willing and drag the unwilling

entries.meaning: Attributed to Lucius Annaeus Seneca (Sen. Ep. 107.11).
entries.author: Lucius Annaeus Seneca

entries.meaning: Motto of the United States Marine Corps Officer Candidates School, at the base in Quantico, Virginia, United States.

common_translation.en: war is sweet to the inexperienced

entries.meaning: Meaning: "war may seem pleasant to those who have never been involved in it, though the experienced know better". Erasmus of Rotterdam.

common_translation.en: It is sweet on occasion to play the fool. / It is pleasant to relax once in a while.

entries.meaning: Horace, Odes 4, 12, 28. Also used by George Knapton for the portrait of Sir Bourchier Wrey, 6th Baronet in 1744.

entries.phrase: dulce et decorum est pro patria mori
entries.normalized: dulce et decorum est pro patria mori
lemma: dulce et decorum est pro patria mori

common_translation.en: It is sweet and honorable to die for the fatherland.

entries.meaning: Horace, Odes 3, 2, 13. Also used by Wilfred Owen for the title of a poem regarding World War I, Dulce et Decorum Est (calling it "the old Lie").

common_translation.en: a sweet and useful thing / pleasant and profitable

entries.meaning: Horace, Ars Poetica: poetry must be dulce et utile, i.e., both enjoyable and instructive.
Horace, Odes, 3 25, 16. Motto of the Scottish clan MacAulay.
Motto of the Scottish clan Fergusson.
Motto of The Ravensbourne School.

common_translation.en: while Rome debates, Saguntum is in danger

entries.meaning: Used when someone has been asked for urgent help, but responds with no immediate action. Similar to Hannibal ante portas, but referring to a less personal danger.
Cicero. Motto of the State of South Carolina. Motto of the Clan MacLennan.

common_translation.en: while there is life, there is hope

entries.meaning: Motto of Presbyterian College.

common_translation.en: while we live, let us live

entries.meaning: An encouragement to embrace life." Emily Dickinson used the line in a whimsical valentine written to William Howland in 1852 and subsequently published in the Springfield Daily Republican:

common_translation.en: he has two, and they dangle nicely

entries.meaning: According to legend, the words spoken by the cardinal verifying that a newly-elected pope was a man, in a test employed after the reign of pope Joan.

common_translation.en: [the] law [is] harsh, but [it is the] law

entries.meaning: A shortening of quod quidem perquam durum est, sed ita lex scripta est ("which indeed is extremely harsh, but thus was the law written"). Ulpian, quoted in the Digesta Iustiniani, Roman jurist of the 3rd century AD.
The outer covering of the brain.
Meaning: "serving at the pleasure of the authority or officer who appointed". A Mediaeval legal Latin phrase.
For example, the Governor General of Canada is durante munere the Chancellor and Principal Companion of the Order of Canada.
description of King Arthur in Historia Brittonum (The History of the Britons); used as title for a 2012 board war game set in the age of King Arthur.

Often used in medicine when the underlying disease causing a symptom is not known. .mw-parser-output div.crossreference{padding-left:0}.mw-parser-output .hatnote{font-style:italic}.mw-parser-output div.hatnote{padding-left:1.6em;margin-bottom:0.5em}.mw-parser-output .hatnote i{font-style:normal}.mw-parser-output .hatnote+link+.hatnote{margin-top:-0.5em}@media print{body.ns-0 .mw-parser-output .hatnote{display:none!important}}See also idiopathic.

Literally, out of more (than one), one. The former national motto of the United States, which "In God We Trust" later replaced; therefore, it is still inscribed on many U.S. coins and on the U.S. Capitol. Also the motto of S.L. Benfica. Less commonly written as ex pluribus unum
John the Baptist exclaims this after seeing Jesus

common_translation.en: behold the handmaiden of the Lord

entries.meaning: From Luke 1:38 in the Vulgate Bible. Name of an oil painting by Dante Gabriel Rossetti and motto of Bishopslea Preparatory School.

From the Gospel of John in the Vulgate 19:5 (Douay-Rheims), where Pontius Pilate speaks these words as he presents Jesus, crowned with thorns, to the crowd. entries.meaning: Roman legal principle formulated by Pomponius in the Digest of the Corpus Juris Civilis, stating that legal actions undertaken by man under the influence of error are invalid.

Sometimes attributed to Seneca the Younger, but not attested: Errare humanum est, perseverare autem diabolicum, et tertia non datur (To err is human; to persist [in committing such errors] is of the devil, and the third possibility is not given.) Several authors contemplated the idea before Seneca: Livy, Venia dignus error is humanus (Storie, VIII, 35) and Cicero: is Cuiusvis errare: insipientis nullius nisi, in errore perseverare (Anyone can err, but only the fool persists in his fault) (Philippicae, XII, 2, 5). Cicero, being well-versed in ancient Greek, may well have been alluding to Euripides' play Hippolytus some four centuries earlier. 300 years later Saint Augustine of Hippo recycled the idea in his Sermones, 164, 14: Humanum fuit errare, diabolicum est per animositatem in errore manere. The phrase gained currency in the English language after Alexander Pope's An Essay on Criticism of 1711: "To err is human, to forgive divine" (line 325).
I. e., mistake. Lists of errors in a previous edition of a work are often marked with the plural errata ("errors").
Motto of Duke University

common_translation.en: to be is to be perceived

entries.meaning: Motto of George Berkeley for his subjective idealist philosophical position that nothing exists independently of its perception by a mind except minds themselves.

common_translation.en: to be, rather than to seem

entries.meaning: Truly being a thing, rather than merely seeming to be a thing. The motto of many institutions. From Cicero, De amicitia (On Friendship), Chapter 26. Prior to Cicero, Sallust used the phrase in Bellum Catilinae, 54, 6, writing that Cato esse quam videri bonus malebat ("preferred to be good, rather than to seem so"). Earlier still, Aeschylus used a similar phrase in Seven Against Thebes, line 592: ou gar dokein aristos, all' enai thelei ("he wishes not to seem the best, but to be the best"). Motto of the State of North Carolina.

there is a middle or mean in things, there is a middle way or position; from Horace, Satires 1.1.106; see also: Golden mean (philosophy). According to Potempski and Galmarini (Atmos. Chem. Phys., 9, 9471–9489, 2009) the sentence should be translated as: "There is an optimal condition in all things", which in the original text is followed by sunt certi denique fines quos ultra citraque nequit consistere rectum ("There are therefore precise boundaries beyond which one cannot find the right thing").

Said of Venice, Italy, by the Venetian historian Fra Paolo Sarpi shortly before his death. Motto of the U.S. state of Idaho, adopted in 1867; of S. Thomas' College, Mount Lavinia, Sri Lanka; of Sigma Phi Society.

entries.phrase: et alii, et aliae, et alia (et al.)
entries.normalized: et alii, et aliae, et alia (et al.)
lemma: et alii, et aliae, et alia (et al.)
entries.meaning: Used similarly to et cetera ("and the rest") to denote names that, usually for the sake of space, are unenumerated/omitted. Alii is masculine, and therefore it can be used to refer to men, or groups of men and women; the feminine et aliae is proper when the "others" are all female, but as with many loanwords, interlingual use, such as in reference lists, is often invariable. Et alia is neuter plural and thus in Latin text is properly used only for inanimate, genderless objects, but some use it as a gender-neutral alternative. APA style and MLA style uses et al. if the work cited was written by more than three authors; AMA style lists all authors if ≤6, and 3 + et al. if >6. AMA style forgoes the period (because it forgoes the period on abbreviations generally) and it forgoes the italic (as it does with other loanwords naturalized into scientific English); many journals that follow AMA style do likewise.
In modern usage, used to mean "and so on" or "and more".

The usual response to the phrase Dominus vobiscum used in Roman Catholic liturgy, for instance at several points during the Catholic Mass. Also used as a general form of greeting among and towards members of Catholic organisations.

However, these were almost certainly not Caesar's true last words: Plutarch quotes Caesar as saying in Greek, the language of the Roman elite at the time, καὶ σὺ τέκνον (Kaì sù téknon?), translated as "You too, (my) child?", quoting from Menander.

"From harmful deceit"; dolus malus is the Latin legal term denoting "fraud". The full legal phrase is ex dolo malo non oritur actio ("an action does not arise from fraud"). When an action has its origin in fraud or deceit, it cannot be supported; thus, a court of law will not assist a man who bases his course of action on an immoral or illegal act.

By virtue or right of office. Often used when someone holds one office by virtue of holding another: for example, the President of France is an ex officio Co-Prince of Andorra. A common misconception is that all ex officio members of a committee or congress may not vote; but in some cases they do. In law ex officio can also refer to an administrative or judicial office taking action of its own accord; in the latter case the more common term is ex proprio motu or ex meru motu, for example to invalidate a patent or prosecute infringers of copyright.

The Catholic Church affirms that the source of grace is God, not just the actions or disposition of the minister or the recipient of the sacrament.

An example of use is in court case titles such as Universal Health Services, Inc. v. United States ex rel. Escobar.

In general, the claim that the absence of something demonstrates the proof of a proposition. An argumentum ex silentio ("argument from silence") is an argument based on the assumption that someone's silence on a matter suggests ("proves" when a logical fallacy) that person's ignorance of the matter or their inability to counterargue validly.

entries.meaning: "Mentioning one thing may exclude another thing". Sometimes expressed as expressum facit cessare tacitum (broadly, "the expression of one thing excludes the implication of something else").

entries.meaning: This expression comes from the Epistle to Jubaianus, paragraph 21, written by Saint Cyprian of Carthage, a bishop of the third century. It is often used to summarise the doctrine that the Catholic Church is absolutely necessary for salvation.

It is issued by the Master of the Papal Liturgical Celebrations before a session of the papal conclave which will elect a new pope. When spoken, all those who are not cardinals, or those otherwise mandated to be present at the conclave, must leave the Sistine Chapel.

entries.normalized: frustra legis auxilium quaerit qui in legem committit
